I am on page 64, item 39, about emotional poverty. So here's what is a surprise about many people. Most people do think of it, for example, the richer somebody is or the more successful somebody is, the less this be true. Essentially that if they are not financially impoverished, if they are not status impoverished, they probably are not emotionally impoverished. The truth of the matter is, it's just about everybody has at least one deep, dark, black hole that they are constantly dragging themselves up out of something that they genuinely don't feel good about. I won't name this person, but I did a fairly extensive video project with a very accomplished and very famous athlete. You would presume, of course, great confidence from this person. First of all, when you move somebody from one place that they are comfortable to another place that they are uncomfortable, often you discover that all of their psychological strengths are situational, not universal. So that happens all the time. We had Johnny Cash on the success events and I had to invent something in a hurry because he's good to speak, right? And he is in the green room, nearly in a fetal position with June Carter Cash trying to talk him down out of the tree. He's put himself in mentally and emotionally. You've got to go out and speak. I mean, you've got to. And he wants no part of this. And I'm going, so I go over. You perform in front of tens of thousands of people all the time. Done it for years. What's, you know, it doesn't matter, by the way, what you do out there. That's not why we got you. They're going to love you no matter what you do. You just go out there and tell some way-long Willy stories. It doesn't have to make a point. Nobody gives a shit about that. They just want to see Johnny Cash. And it's 20 stinking minutes. I can't do it. He said, what do you mean you can't do it? He said, I looked out to Curtin. There's all those people. I'll freeze up. I won't be able to speak. This is Johnny Cash. I'm going, would you feel better if you took your guitar with you? Yeah, I'd feel better if I took my guitar. That's incredible. So you have your guitar. And then I invented. And then we used it, actually a fair amount. I said, OK, you're not going to speak. Peter's going to interview you. And you are just going to look at Peter. That's all you're going to do. Just never look down. Look at Peter. Answer the questions and strum your guitar anytime you feel like it. And he was fine, by the way. So that's situational. But this particular athlete, it turns out, was not situational. This particular athlete, despite being an incredible competitor, actually was forcing himself to do that on a moment-to-moment basis. And it may be the most fragile person I've ever worked with. And it doubled the length of time necessary to do the work. Because I now am, I don't dare criticize. That second take is all got to be couched elaborately. And the man, we got it. It's like perfect. I just want to see if anything cool happens when we do it a second time. I'm doing this, right? I can't be saying, Jesus, you blew two lines. And the voice inflection on this was no good. Let's do it again. And can you give it to me this way? You couldn't do it. You couldn't do it. You never got through this. There's a very wealthy person, not Trump, by the way. There's got to be a deep, dark hole there somewhere. But there's a very wealthy person who I've done a lot of work with, who has a great front to the public, but is an emotional basket case. And so my theory is that everybody has at least one. They have one. And this now, not coincidentally, is really their greatest hidden desire. So this is the thing that they really would love to feel better about. And actually, a lot of what they spend money on, and a lot of things they do, and a lot of places they go, is really about that. They rarely enunciate it to anybody else. And they probably, in many cases, don't enunciate it to themselves. But it is there. Do you remember Sally Field at the Oscars? I do not believe it was contrived. I think it was a spontaneous, irresistible outburst. It was, if you don't remember, Sally literally kind of crazily screaming, you like me, you really like me, waving the trophy. And I strongly suspect this is a person who, for whatever reasons, I don't know her, have never worked with her, is disliked and or disrespected a lot. And so if you think about the things that she's been most excited, she's, most people know her either as the flying nun, if you go back far enough, or they know her from Smokey and the Bandit movie. You know, not from her great dramatic performances. And so I think her primal scream is waiting inside everybody about something. And if you can figure it out, either for the individual or in common for a group of individuals. So for example, with chiropractors, it has always been easy to hit them in how disrespected they are comparatively to real doctors. In fact, that's about all you got to do to set them off. And by the way, if you do it by mistake and you do it undiliberately, you lose them all. They're like gone. They might have climbed for a number of years. A catalog publisher and they sell a bunch of stuff, add specialties and whatever to doctors. And they started in dentistry. They're very big in dentistry. And they had MDs as well. And so the first catalog they sent to chiropractors, they got like nothing. And they couldn't figure it out. I look at the catalog and a third of the examples, like the imprinted post-it notes, reactivation notices with Garfield the cat on them. The sample they're showing is Dr. Harry Williams' MD. Chiropractor went, if this, I don't want anything to do with these people. They deal with MDs. Trash, right? So if you use it wrong, it's a problem. But if you use it right, I've used now five or six times over the years for different marketers to chiro's. Most recently, I used it with the pharmacy owners. But the chiro one is this photograph. And it's got, you know how you got a Walmart? It's my number one gripe about Walmart. Is there's reserve parking for everybody but me, right? I mean, there's three spots reserved for pregnant women. There's 15 spots reserved for handicapped people. There's a spot for the employee of the month. There's a spot for the disadvantaged transgender. There's all these signs, right? And I'm parking way the hell over there, right? And so we have this picture with parking signs, VIP, MD, VIP, DDS, VIP, everything. And then a sign with DC on it for Chiro and a big no red symbol on it. And that's our parking lot. And it just triggers all sorts of, you know, emotions. And their real hidden desire is to be respected as a real doctor. That's what's going on inside them. And when you can find it, you can really push that button. People are really susceptible. All right, let's get into some real interesting stuff. Starting with item 42, we'll talk about optimistic biases and we'll talk about addiction. We'll talk about sunk in cost pressure. We'll talk about stupidity. So almost everybody by preference, the most pessimistic people and most cynical people, perhaps you know, are still susceptible to optimistic bias. So consider the lottery. The lottery, first of all, it's a tax on poor people that cannot be taxed any other way. That's first of all what it is. However, almost 40% of all lottery buyers have household incomes in excess of 50 grand a year. So it is not all poor people buying. Almost 10% of the lottery players spend over $500 a week. So there are whales, there are heavy hitters. Of a client, you may not know this business exists, a client I've been doing a lot of work for over the past year, they're called Win Trillions. You can find them at wintrillions.com. Hell, why say millions? So Win Trillions is the largest equivalent of E-Trade for lotteries. So instead of playing just your local lottery, you can play any and every lottery in the world because they'll buy tickets for you in any lottery there is. So Powerball, of course, is universal, global. Most of the foreign buyers want to play our Powerball. But Spain has this big giant lottery and Italy has a lottery. And then there's some lotteries that never really pay big jackpots, but they pay out a lot of prizes, small prizes to a lot of people. And so Win Trillions is a buyers agent for lottery players who have them buy tickets for them. And they also bundle people into groups. So if you're going to spend $100 this week, instead of having $100 in the Illinois lottery, Harry, you can have one-one-millionth of a share for your $100 in tickets in 1,000 lotteries, which sounds, by the way, like you've just enormously increased your likelihood of winning. It doesn't. But it sounds like that. It's kind of like Bob Stupak at Vegas World, one of the games he had was FaceUp Blackjack. You saw the dealer's cards. And because of a few other rule changes, your odds of winning were actually worse than in regular Blackjack, multi-deck. Yeah, crapless craps, if you know anything about craps. Every number was a point number. You couldn't crap out on the first roll. Now you would think, right? Wow. It makes it worse. Because try and hit a two again. So this is really the same thing. There's a lot of presumption in the marketing that people think. So there's these pools. There's auto buy, so you don't forget to play your numbers. So they just whack your card and buy for you so you never forget. If you happen to be in a car accident and go into a coma, it's OK. Your lottery tickets are still going to get bought. So A, this is a very big business, tiny margin, by the way, but a very big business. But their stats track the overall stats. About 10% of their players are whales. They're playing serious money. But all people who, so the Ohio's lottery slogan for years has been, you can't win if you don't play. Of course you can't win if you do play either. But you can't win if you don't play. Which prima facia is true, right? And where the optimistic bias comes in is somebody does win. And the fact that somebody does win triumphs over the facts of the odds of you winning. Which by the way, the bigger the jackpot, the less likely you are of winning. So people behave double stupid because people who never play it regularly run out when the jackpot hits a certain point and they play. The worst time, if you insist on playing, you play the week after they've just paid out $12 billion when the pool is small. But all of this is, you know, the odds are so stacked against you by law they publish them. The news media does a fairly good job of saying to people, hey, Powerball's up to this and your chances of winning are one in 500 million people. You know, it's right up there. We get struck by lightning 17 times in the same day. You know, at a picnic where Clinton and Trump are hanging out together. You know, it's right there, right? But the optimistic bias still sets in. Now a bunch of people will say to you, right, I just play for fun. I have no real expectation of winning. Really? Right? Really? So why do you check the numbers? Well, to see if I won. Oh, oh, so you did think you might win. Well, no, not really. Well then why do you check the numbers? Right? Well, to see if I won. Oh, okay. Right? And when do you check them? Really is possible moment. 10 o'clock news. Right there. Ticket in hand. Numbers, it's not throw it in the car and check it the next time at the convenience. No, no, no, no, no, no, no. It's right now. So that optimistic bias is in place for something for which for most people it should not be. It just should not be. So this filters its way into all sorts of things, all sorts of behavior, and into repetitive behavior just like playing the lottery is. So I have a friend and she goes to visit her offspring about three times a year. Has been doing it, I don't know, 10 years. Every time she goes with a set of expectations of what this visit is going to be like. Some of you in the room will identify with this perfectly. And every time it is not like that. And what it is like is very consistent. It's the same every time. But somehow this time it's going to be different. You all have a relative who is a complete screw up, if not worse. And you will repetitively, with time gaps usually, but repetitively you will be sucked into dispensing free advice and trying to help this person. And the result is the same every single time. And yet sense of obligation, blood, a number of things, but optimistic bias sets in. This time it's going to be different. Women, people think it's all poor women, it is not. Women who could afford to leave stay in very abusive relationships for many, many, many years before they finally either die, wind up dead, or get out. Because he only hits me when he's drunk and loses his temper. And he says he'll never do it again. You know what the statistical odds are of a second time after a first, anybody know? 92%. 92%. First time, pack a shed, get out. But they don't. So optimistic bias is there, it's in everybody's personality, it's in more than it is in some, certainly, but it is there in everybody. Some people own self images, make it more profound. So the person who takes self image pride in being an optimist, in being a positive thinker, they obligate themselves to high optimistic bias. So this is why the same fundamental thing can be sold to people over and over and over again. Take diets. So the odds are about the same, by the way. Once somebody buys one diet, there's a 92% chance they're buying a second one. And there's a very high chance they're going to be addicts. So it becomes, it moves into optimistic addiction. They are going to buy the next diet, and if they're buying pills, they're going to buy pills. And if they're buying shots, they're going to buy shots. If they're buying plans like Weight Watchers, they're going to buy another plan, they're going to buy another plan. 30% of Weight Watchers business is done with people who are on Weight Watchers for between the third and the fifth time. So they go on a circle. They do this one, they do this one, they do this one. They wind up back with the one they already failed on, and they buy it again anyway. Because they're locked into optimistic bias addiction. Opportunity seekers very much this way. So they will, and they'll buy even the same opportunity that they have done nothing with three or four times. So if you take all, if we could get the list of all the Trump University victims, and we could cross it with a Bob Allen list, a Ron LeGrand list, a Kiyosaki Get Rich and Real Estate List, I promise you, the dupes are better than half. I mean duplicates, I don't mean dupes. Because truth is, any of that stuff works if you follow the instructions and do it. It's not quite as easy as it's made out to be, but what is? Right? If anybody went to jail for whatever it is they're pitching, not being as easy as they made it out to be, we'd all be in jail. Our spouses would all be in jail. So this bias, well you go back and check those vows. So this bias is there. People want to, they are even, not hardwired, but it's deeply embedded, optimistic bias. We do it to our kids. Santa Claus. We bias. And lots of kids ask for shit, they don't get that shit. Particularly poor kids. They ask for stuff and they get socks and underwear and, you know. They still make out a list next year. It takes a long time before they stop making out the list. Even now, with access to the internet and all that, still, Macy still has the mailbox in the store every year for letters to Santa and people still go put letters to Santa in. Right? The internet hasn't killed it yet. You would think, right? You would think people who pray the same way if they pray by making a list for Santa. Hey God, here's a list of shit I want. And when they don't get it, it takes a long time before they quit praying. My mother-in-law, you know, is a vout Catholic. That's the way she prayed. To the best of my knowledge, she never got what she prayed for. She never stopped praying. So it's in there, pretty deep again. And so it can be stimulated. So now you loop back. See when they see, so think about the lottery. So they know somebody wins. Why? Because a lot of people know they've got to show off the winners. Right? That's proof. Right? They use statistics. We've paid out $200 million in winnings so far this year. That's a big number. Nobody goes, well, what could they have paid out $200 million? Casinos do it. You walk into a casino, there'll be a sign. They've paid out the places that sell lottery tickets. There's a Sonoco station over by where I sometimes I get gas there. They still have the pen and up from the winner. I think $400 and $700,000 to bought their lottery ticket there. In 2009, they spray that pen and off every day because it helps sell lottery tickets on which they get a commission. Right? So again, all this stuff, Jeff was asking me this one. All this stuff knits together. So the optimistic bias is there to be triggered. How do you trigger it? You trigger it with a bunch of these other things and you know that's what you're trying to trigger. Sunk and cost. It's best to sell things to people who have a huge sunk and cost investment in whatever it is you are selling them things for. It happens, but for the most part, married people who go see a therapist have been married for a decade or longer. They have a huge sunk and cost investment in that marriage. So they buy things to try and fix it. Six months, they get an annulment. There's a big movement, by the way. I don't know if you've noticed it in the news, but it's millennial driven that we should be in the expired date marriage business. These licenses should be issued with automatic expired dates. So there's no, I'm serious. This is a big discussion. That it's antiquated the way it is and the way to eliminate all this acrimony and fighting over who gets what and people forgetting to do a pre-nop and all that shit is just to have it expire after three years. It's over. Everybody knows it's going to be over. Everybody packs up their stuff and goes merrily on their way. So it's why in B2B, see, I'd rather sell to a dentist than to a real estate agent. Why? The dentist has a big sunk and cost investment. He went to school. So for you, particularly now, because you've been a dentist for a long time, but even if you haven't been a dentist for, for you to wake up in the morning and say, screw it, I don't want to be a dentist anymore. That's a big deal. You got an office. Maybe you built the building. Maybe you leased it. You got a bunch of equipment in there. You got staff. You went to school. There is an income. You know, you go home to your wife and say, I've decided not to be a dentist anymore. I'm just going to go shut the damn thing down. And I think I want to go sell boats. That's why the conversation almost never happens. But if it does happen, the conversation ends with you are not going and sell boats. That's how that conversation ends, right? Unhappy real estate agent. What's he got? Got a website. If our guys were good at selling stuff to him, he's got a website. He's got a cell phone, which he's going to keep anyway. And he's got a business card. And he went to, how long you got to go to school to get a license? 40 hours. So he went to school for 40 hours in a day to get his license. That's his investment. He says, I think I'll go sell boats. Great. Maybe you'll do better selling boats. She says, and off he goes. Right? So how invested we are in something has a great deal to do with how we work. We will respond to anything related to it. I could have got out of a horse business real easy when I had one. Even two. Maybe even three. 23. There's nice people who employees who depend on me. There in three different places. There's relationships. There's ego tied to it now. There's habit tied to it now. I can't get out. Therefore I'm much more susceptible to any and everything that might help it. But I had one, you are not going to sell me a balance therapy thing that they stand on and it vibrates under their feet for 15 minutes a day and it somehow stimulates their lymph nodes. It's a big story. We got one now. And we got a guy getting paid to stand there with them to make sure they don't get off. Right? Guy selling that thing shows up when I got one. I laugh at him. Guy shows up when we got 20. I go get it off the truck. You know? So Greg also on Tars horse says the same thing. New sulky. Right? Yeah. Okay. Driver. Right? If you like to drive once a year at the fair, at your hometown fair, you'll have the same sulky for life. And it's going to be the old wood one with bigger wheels. If you're driving a lot, first of all there's comparative status going on. There's positional. So as soon as one of the top drivers gets a new sulky, which they give to them, everybody else has got to have that new sulky. The one you got is not good enough anymore and maybe his goes this much faster. Wheels. Oh shit. New speed wheels. We got to get new speed wheels. How much are those? 800 dollars. No problem. Right? So it's all about how invested you already are in something now drives.
